data.title: ICE under fire: The problem isn't what it's doing, it's how
data.description:
Minneapolis is in a state of chaos, perhaps even immersed in civil unrest. Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers have saturated the area, with around 3,000 ICE officers expected once all are fully deployed. Yet the ideal of what ICE is intended to do — support legal immigration — does not match the “how” of what they are executing.

The polarization of the U.S. into two factions is dry timber for such a conflagration. Actions taken by ICE officers are exposing such schisms. This bifurcation can be seen from the nation’s House of Representatives, where it is estimated that fewer than 40 districts out of 435 will be competitive in this year’s midterm elections — lowest number in the last 40 years. This lack of competitiveness is due to congressional map gerrymandering in numerous states, as well as the clustering of people of like mind. Both are symptomatic of an environment that is breaking our nation apart socially, much like what civil wars do on a battlefield. 

The recent ICE activities in the Minneapolis area demonstrate how a federal law enforcement agency can overwhelm a community, instilling fear among its residents. A picture of a person dressed in a clown outfit is representative of the non-violent approaches being used to demonstrate displeasure. Regrettably, more aggressive attacks have also surfaced as tensions escalate. 

Note that some people are supportive of ICE and their tactics in Minneapolis. This has surfaced with confrontations between pro-ICE and anti-ICE efforts, creating additional public discord. 

Nothing goes unseen in public today. In our digital age, where everyone has a cellphone, recordings of ICE activity are both ubiquitous and legal. When Renee Good was shot by an ICE officer this month, numerous videos showed her pulling her vehicle away from, not driving at, the ICE officer involved. In response, the Department of Homeland Security released a statement that the ICE officer suffered internal bleeding after being struck by Goode’s vehicle, even though creditable, on-the-scene video evidence to support such claims has yet to be released.

How people interpret these videos may be widely different, but the volume of videos taken from different angles provides a visual account of what transpired, allowing anyone to make their own assessment.

If ICE had the manpower, it would likely be saturating every community around the nation, using similar tactics to what it is using in Minneapolis. This has stoked protests around the nation, showing support for their brethren in Minneapolis, but perhaps also knowing what is happening in Minneapolis could also occur where they live. 

Most people agree that immigration is an issue of concern for our nation. Finding ways to secure the nation’s borders while treating all people humanely and with respect is necessary and appropriate. Yet a statement issued by the Department of Homeland Security on the activities of ICE officers as they search for “criminal illegal alien murderer(s) and sexual predator(s)” suggests they are not just seeking those who have committed crimes. When they instead seek people who have dark skin, their mission morphs into what appears to be racial profiling. 

Numerous instances have been reported where both U.S. citizens and documented immigrants are being approached, questioned and apprehended by ICE. Given that such officers carry military-style weapons, this creates an ominous presentation when they gather together in search of suspects. Moreover, this creates an atmosphere that undermines some of the principles embedded in the Bill of Rights, including protecting individual freedoms and placing limits on government power. 

If ICE continues its tactics in Minneapolis, and perhaps expands its efforts to other cities, and Congress does nothing to rein it in, protests will continue and grow in both size and scope. Indeed, violent responses are being instigated by how the administration is acting, not just by what it states ICE agents’ assignment to be. 

President Trump once posted on social media, “He who saves his Country does not violate any Law.” What is being executed by ICE in Minneapolis appears to be emblematic of this belief. Without the full gamut of governmental levers exercising its authority, ICE’s intolerable actions will continue, much to the detriment of the people of Minneapolis and the nation as a whole. Sadly, it also demonstrates to the world that the U.S. no longer holds a moral compass that can be trusted.     

Sheldon H. Jacobson, Ph.D., is a computer science professor in the Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. As a data scientist, he uses his expertise in risk-based analytics to address issues impacting public policy.

data.title: House Democratic leaders to oppose DHS bill over ICE funding
data.description:
The top House Democrats are lining up to oppose legislation funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), largely to protest the financing of Trump’s immigration crackdown in blue regions around the country.

During a closed-door meeting of the House Democratic Caucus in the basement of the Capitol on Wednesday, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) and Rep. Katherine Clark (Mass.), the Democratic whip, announced that they would vote against the DHS bill when it hits the floor on Thursday, according to several sources in the meeting. 

Their opposition reflects a growing sentiment among Democrats that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is abusing its powers in cracking down on those living in the country illegally. Those criticisms have exploded following this month’s fatal shooting of an unarmed woman by a federal immigration officer in Minneapolis, which has sparked an outcry from Democrats clamoring for tougher rules governing the conduct of ICE officers. 

Asked if he will support the bill, Rep. Gabe Vasquez (D-N.M.) summed up the Democratic mood.

“Hell no,” he said.

The opposition is not expected to sink the bill. Democrats are the minority party, and even if most of them vote against it, there are likely to be enough centrist lawmakers who cross the aisle and sided with the majority of Republicans to get it across the finish line. 

Rep. Henry Cuellar (Texas) is one of them. He’s the top Democrat on the Appropriations Committee’s Homeland Security subpanel, and he helped negotiate the bipartisan DHS bill. He was quick to acknowledge that Democrats didn’t get everything they wanted, in terms of ICE restrictions, but said the reforms they did negotiate are worthy of support. 

“We wanted more oversight,” he said after Wednesday’s meeting. “But, look, Democrats don’t control the House, we don’t control the Senate or the White House. But we were able to add some oversight over Homeland.”

Rep. Rosa DeLauro (Conn.), the top Democrat on the Appropriations Committee, also made the case that a failure to pass a new DHS spending bill is the worse option because it would cause either a partial shutdown or a short-term stopgap bill, which would not include the ICE reforms Democrats negotiated. 

“The CR gives all of the control to the president and Russell Vought,” DeLauro said. 

Still, she acknowledged the levels of outrage within the Democratic Caucus, and declined to commit to voting for the bill herself. 

“There’s a lot of anger about Homeland … and rightly so,” she said.

data.title: New study reveals political affiliations of pro athletes
data.description:
A new study offers insight into the political affiliation of professional athletes, showing a wide spectrum of opinions on current events across different sports.

The VoteHub study found 53 percent of Major League Baseball players are registered Republicans, the highest proportion of any league, while 43 percent of players in the National Hockey League said the same.

The NHL had the highest share of independents at 48.6 percent of the league, followed by the National Football League, where 44 percent have registered with no party. Just over 34 percent of NFL players were registered Democrats and just over 20 percent were registered Republicans.

The most registered Democrats of any major pro sports league came in the WNBA, where a whopping 67 percent of athletes supported the party. The second bluest league was the NBA, where almost 43 percent of players identify as Democrats and 10 percent as Republicans.

“When combining the data of the five leagues, the share of independents is 41 percent, higher than the national average of 27 percent,” the study said. “This could indicate that political engagement is lower, or that athletes are hiding their true affiliations with the awareness that they are public figures.”

VoteHub relied on publicly available voter registration data from 24 states and the District of Columbia.

data.title: Vance to visit Minneapolis amid immigration crackdown tensions
data.description:
Vice President JD Vance is set to visit Minneapolis on Thursday amid large-scale protests over the Trump administration’s crackdown on undocumented immigrants in the area. 

Vance will speak with business owners and community leaders, a source familiar with the trip told NewsNation, The Hill’s sister network. 

The planned visit follows sparks of public outrage in response to the killing of Renee Good, a mother and Minneapolis resident who was shot by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer after ignoring requests to step out of her vehicle. 

Good attempted to drive away and was fatally wounded by officer Jonathan Ross. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Ross’s actions would undergo internal review, but originally defended him, alleging that Good was using her vehicle as a “deadly weapon.”

The Trump administration quickly labeled Good as a “domestic terrorist” who refused to comply with authorities. Vance said Ross would be granted “absolute immunity” while speaking from the lectern at a recent White House press briefing.

He also noted that Good was part of a “broader left-wing network to attack, to dox, to assault and to make it impossible for ICE officers to do their job.”

data.title: California Republicans ask Supreme Court to block new House map
data.description:
California Republicans are requesting the Supreme Court block a new Democratic-favored House map from going into effect ahead of the November elections.

The California GOP is appealing a decision made by a lower court last week that blocked Republicans’ bid to stop a new set of congressional lines from being put in place for the midterms. The map, which was approved by voters in November, would offer Democrats up to five pickup opportunities.

Republicans allege that Democrats created the map as an illegal racial gerrymander that maximized Latino voting power instead of for partisan intent, an argument the federal court rejected last week.

Republicans want the high court to issue a ruling by Feb. 9 on their request to have the new Democratic-favored House map paused for the 2026 cycle. If that were to happen, the state would go back to its original map for November while the larger questions concerning the redistricting ballot measure work their way through the courts.

“Unlike in Texas, where the mapmaker testified under oath at length to explain the non-racial considerations that informed his district line-drawing,” the plaintiffs wrote, “the mapmaker here publicly acknowledged that he used race to design Proposition 50’s districts but then invoked a broad claim of legislative privilege to avoid testifying about his work or his public acknowledgements of his racial purpose during the preliminary injunction proceedings.”

“The legislators for whom he worked similarly invoked legislative privilege to avoid testifying with respect to the drawing of Proposition 50’s lines,” the plaintiffs added. “This has left various statements establishing a racial purpose in the drawing of the district lines unrebutted.”

Republicans and Democrats have been mired in a redistricting battle ever since Texas Republicans moved last year to redraw a new House map that offers the GOP up to five pickup opportunities.

That prompted California Democrats to tee up a special election last November where voters overwhelmingly passed a new Democratic-favored House map that effectively nixes potential Texas GOP gains.

In a separate challenge, the Supreme Court allowed Texas Republicans to use their new map in time for the midterm elections while the merits of the case play out.

In that emergency ruling, the court suggested that both California and Texas created their maps for partisan intent, meaning it’s unclear if the high court will be persuaded by the argument California Republicans are making here.

However, a separate case the Supreme Court is separately expected to make a decision on – Louisiana’s congressional maps and the Voting Rights Act – could have a major impact on this decision. The high court signaled during an unusual rehearing of a case in October that it was likely to limit the use of race in redistricting, which could have potential consequences for the decision over California’s House map.

data.title: UK's Starmer rips Trump for backtracking on Chagos Islands deal support
data.description:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Wednesday went after President Trump for reversing position on a deal surrounding the Chagos Islands, suggesting the president did so because of tensions over his plan to acquire Greenland.

“I made out my position on Greenland absolutely clear on Monday and a moment ago. President Trump deployed words on Chagos yesterday that were different to his previous words of welcome and support when I met him in the White House,” Starmer said in the House of Commons.

“He deployed those words yesterday for the express purpose of putting pressure on me and Britain in relation to my values and principles on the future of Greenland,” he added, emphasizing he would not give in.

Both the U.S. and U.K. operate a joint naval and bomber base in the Chagos Islands, which is in the Indian Ocean.

On Tuesday, Trump went after the U.K. over yielding control of the Chagos Islands to Mauritius, which the Trump administration had previously supported. In the deal, Mauritius would regain power over the islands and the U.K. would pay an average of $136 million to the Mauritians to lease back the island of Diego Garcia for a minimum of 99 years.

The president said on Truth Social early Tuesday that the U.K. was handing over Diego Garcia “FOR NO REASON WHATSOEVER.”

“There is no doubt that China and Russia have noticed this act of total weakness,” Trump continued. “These are International Powers who only recognize STRENGTH, which is why the United States of America, under my leadership, is now, after only one year, respected like never before.”

Trump’s Tuesday post came amid tensions with European allies, including the U.K., over his quest to acquire Greenland. The Arctic island is a territory of Denmark, which is a member of NATO alongside the U.S. and the U.K.

Starmer on Monday criticized Trump, saying tariffs targeting countries defending Greenland were “completely wrong.”

“Any decision about the future status of Greenland belongs to the people of Greenland and the kingdom of Denmark alone,” the prime minister said at a press conference.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Give consumers the option to use cash for purchases
data.description:
Before I ever came to Congress, I spent my days on the construction site, earning paychecks the same way millions of Americans do. I learned the value of a dollar early in life, and I still remember my first paycheck. That perspective stays with you.

It is also why I was stunned when I tried to buy some ice cream with cash in the U.S. Capitol and was told they do not accept cash. The idea that the U.S. Capitol does not accept U.S. currency would make anyone pause. If cash is not good enough there, where else is it quietly being pushed aside?

About 4.5 percent of U.S. households do not have checking or savings accounts, and cash still accounts for nearly 20 percent of all payments nationwide.

In Washington, we spend a lot of time arguing over complicated problems with no easy answers. But every once in a while, an issue comes along where the solution is simple and the public overwhelmingly agrees. Protecting purchasers’ right to use cash is one of those issues.

A new survey from the Siena Research Institute makes that clear. More than four in five Americans oppose turning the U.S. into a fully cashless society. That support cuts across party lines. Republicans, Democrats and Independents all agree that people should be able to pay with the money they earn.

When businesses refuse to accept cash, it takes away that basic choice. For many Americans — especially tipped and low-wage workers — cash is what they have on hand. They shouldn’t be told their money isn’t good enough. These workers deserve to be able to pay with their tipped cash just as easily as paying with a credit card.

Digital payments can be helpful, but they are not free. Fees, interest charges and budgeting challenges add up, especially at a time when grocery prices and everyday costs keep climbing. In today’s economy, the last thing families need is another hidden fee.

Declining cash payments is more than just an inconvenience; it’s about fairness. About 25 million households in this country either do not have bank accounts or use non-bank products to meet their core financial needs. For them, cash isn’t a preference: it’s often the only option. When it comes to buying groceries, filling a prescription, and paying for transportation, a cashless economy will shut millions of Americans out of everyday life.

That’s why I joined Rep. John Rose (R-Tenn.) in introducing the bipartisan Payment Choice Act. The bill would require most brick-and-mortar businesses to accept cash for in-person purchases up to $500. According to the Siena survey, 85 percent of Americans support that kind of common-sense requirement.

Right now, it feels like Washington can’t agree on much of anything. But this is an issue where the public is clear. People want choice. They want fairness. And they want the freedom to decide how they spend the money they earned.

Donald Norcross represents New Jersey’s 1st Congressional District in the U.S. House of Representatives.

data.title: Trump threats challenge Europe security, prosperity: Top EU official
data.description:
European Council President António Costa said on Wednesday that President Trump’s threats to impose tariffs and take control of Greenland threaten the continent’s security and prosperity.

“The geopolitical challenges Europe is facing sometimes seems daunting: Russia’s war of aggression against Ukraine, the international rules-based order being undermined, core alliances being questioned, but the European Union will come out of this stronger, more resilient and more sovereign,” Costa said in remarks to the European Parliament on Wednesday.

“For this to happen, our response must have three components: a Europe of principles, a Europe of protection and a Europe of prosperity,” he added. “All these three dimensions are being tested in the current moment of transatlantic relations.”

Costa said he decided to convene Thursday’s emergency summit of the EU’s 27 national leaders to address these challenges.

The meeting comes as tensions between the U.S. and European allies have escalated dramatically in recent days, as the Trump administration continues to insist on the U.S. acquiring Greenland — a mineral-rich Danish territory in the Arctic — for national security purposes.

The president responded to Europe’s strong opposition to his push by unveiling new tariffs this weekend on Denmark and several European countries. He said 10 percent tariffs would take effect on Feb. 1, rising to 25 percent on June 1, unless a deal is reached.

Trump wrote on Truth Social that the tariffs “will be due and payable until such time as a Deal is reached for the Complete and Total purchase of Greenland.”

But Costa maintained that the EU would not back down from its defense of Greenland, opposition to new tariffs and commitment to the NATO alliance.

Ahead of the summit, the European Council chief outlined several guiding principles that he believes are “broadly shared” among members and that he hopes will guide the discussion.

“First, we are united around the principles of international law, territorial integrity and national sovereignty,” Costa said. “Second, we are united in full support and solidarity with the Kingdom of Denmark and with Greenland. Only they, Denmark and Greenland, can decide on their future.”

He added that European leaders also agree “further tariffs would undermine transatlantic relations and are incompatible with the EU-US agreement,” which was struck last July but whose fate has been called into question after Trump’s tariff threat.

Costa said members agree that “there is a shared transatlantic interest in peace and security in the Arctic, notably working through NATO,” but noted that European countries “stand ready to defend ourselves, our member states, our citizens, our companies, against any form of coercion.”

“And the European Union has the power and the tools to do so,” he added.

Finally, Costa said Europeans leaders want to “continue engaging constructively with United States, of course, on all issues of common interest, and there are many, since we are partners and allies and share a transatlantic community.”

Trump is set to meet with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte and European allies on Wednesday on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

data.title: Israel, Kosovo join Trump's Gaza peace board
data.description:
Israel and Kosovo announced Wednesday that they have accepted invitations to join President Trump’s Board of Peace in Gaza.

“Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has announced that he accepts the invitation of US President Donald Trump and will become a member of the Board of Peace, which is to be comprised of world leaders,” a statement from the Israeli prime minister’s office stated.

It marks a reversal for the Israeli government, which initially objected to the Board of Peace, stating that the composition was “contrary to its policy,” particularly due to the inclusion of regional adversaries like Turkey. Its membership is also controversial, as Israel would be helping to oversee the fragile ceasefire it is directly involved in with Hamas.

Vjosa Osmani, the president of Kosovo, posted a photo of Trump’s letter online and wrote that she was “deeply honored” by the president’s personal invitation.

“True leadership doesn’t just talk about peace, it delivers it,” Osmani wrote in a post on social platform X. “That is exactly what @POTUS Trump is doing through this historic initiative.”

“America helped bring peace to Kosovo. Today, Kosovo stands firmly as America’s ally, ready to help carry that peace forward. History remembers those who take bold steps to build peace – and we are ready,” she added.

Trump is set to chair the Board of Peace, which will be tasked with supervising postwar restoration efforts in Gaza as part of the second phase of the president’s 20-point peace plan.

More than a dozen countries, now including Israel and Kosovo, have agreed to sign on to the charter, according to The Associated Press. The list includes Argentina, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Bahrain, Belarus, Egypt, Hungary, Kazakhstan, Morocco, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam.

Heads of state from multiple other nations, including Russian President Vladimir Putin and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, have also reportedly been asked to join the board, though many have remained noncommittal.

At least one country, France, has flatly rejected the invitation, stating that it does not plan to join “at this stage,” amid concerns that the charter could undermine the framework of the United Nations.

Trump responded by threatening to implement a 200 percent tariff on French wine and champagne, but brushed off French President Emmanuel Macron’s refusal to join the board.

“I’ll put a 200 percent tariff on his wines and Champagnes, and he’ll join. But he doesn’t have to join,” he told reporters on Tuesday, later adding, “he’s going to be out of office in a few months.”

The president is also facing criticism following multiple reports that he will ask countries to pay a $1 billion membership fee for a permanent spot on the board.  

The White House has pushed back on the reporting as “misleading.”

“There is no minimum membership fee to join the Board of Peace,” the administration’s rapid response X account posted last Saturday. “This simply offers permanent membership to partner countries who demonstrate deep commitment to peace, security, and prosperity.”

data.title: NATO chief: 'No way' European economies would have grown so much without Trump
data.description:
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte on Wednesday credited President Trump for Europe’s economic growth, arguing there was “no way” it would have happened without the new president.

“I mean, many of you, I know, criticize Donald Trump. But do you really think that without Donald Trump, eight big economies in Europe, including Spain, Italy, and Belgium, Canada, by the way, also outside Europe, would have come to 2 percent in 2025 when they were only on 1.5 percent at the beginning of the year? No way,” Rutte said during the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

“Without Donald Trump, this would never have happened,” added Rutte, who has used compliments for Trump as he has pressed the U.S. president to back NATO.

The NATO chief’s praise for Trump was rampant last year ahead of the NATO summit in June where the alliance successfully agreed to spend 5 percent of GDP on defense spending.

“Do you really think that in the summit in The Hague, as Alex was already saying, sorry, the President of Finland is saying that we would come to an overall defense spending of 5 percent, including 3.5 percent in core defense if President Trump would not have been re-elected as President of the United States. No way! It would never have happened,” Rutte said.

“So again, I’m not popular with you now because I’m defending Donald Trump, but I really believe you can be happy that he is there because he has forced us in Europe to step up, to face the consequences that we have to take care more of our own defense,” he continued. 

Trump’s Wednesday address at the Davos meetings comes amid high tensions between the administration and many of its western allies over Trump’s efforts to acquire Greenland, which is now a territory of Denmark’s.

Trump in his speech said he would not take Greenland by force, a significant shift in that he previously had not taken force off the table. But Trump made it clear that aquiring Greenland remained important to him and to U.S. national security, in his view.

Rutte has also emphasized the importance of Greenland to NATO security.

“We need to defend the Arctic. We know that the sea lanes are opening up. We know that China and Russia are increasingly active in the Arctic. There are eight countries bordering on the Arctic. Seven are members of NATO. That’s Finland, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Iceland, Canada, and the US. And there’s only one country bordering on the Arctic outside NATO, and that’s Russia,” Rutte said. 

“And I would argue there is a ninth country, which is China, which is increasingly active in the Arctic region. So, President Trump and other leaders are right, we have to do more there, we have to protect the Arctic.”
